worth_it_explanation: The attribution setting decides which conversions Meta counts as ad-driven and which conversions feed delivery, so a window that does not match how people actually buy distorts both the report and the optimization at the same time. The check is fast. The setting sits on each ad set and shows in a reporting column, so reading it across an account takes one pass. The fix is a configuration change at ad set creation plus a habit of comparing tools on the same window, not a rebuild, and it changes the numbers from the next reporting pull.
intro: An attribution setting in Meta Ads Manager pairs a click window with a view window, for example 7-day click and 1-day view, and tells Meta how many days after a click or a view a conversion can still be credited to the ad. The setting lives on the ad set, and the same window that reports a conversion is the window that informs delivery. These checks confirm that the window matches the account's real buying cycle, that comparisons against other tools account for the window, and that view-through credit is read for what it is rather than treated as click-driven performance.

audit_checks.title: The attribution window is left at default and does not match the buying cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A window shorter than the real consideration period drops conversions that the ad did influence, and a window longer than the cycle claims credit for conversions that would have happened anyway. Either way the reported volume is off and delivery optimizes toward the wrong signal, so spend follows numbers that do not reflect how the audience buys.

educate.base:
An attribution setting in Meta Ads Manager is the click window and view window applied to an ad set, for example 7-day click and 1-day view. It sets how many days after someone clicks or views an ad a conversion can still be credited to that ad. The setting does two jobs at once. It decides which conversions appear in the report, and it decides which conversions Meta uses to learn what is working and steer delivery.

Newly created ad sets start on a default window, so a window chosen by Meta rather than by the buying cycle is what shapes the count until someone changes it. A store selling a low-priced impulse item and a business selling a service with a multi-week sales cycle both inherit the same starting window even though their buyers behave nothing alike.

A window that is too short for the cycle drops conversions the ad genuinely helped drive when the purchase lands after the window closes. A window that is too long for the cycle credits conversions that would have happened without the ad, pulling in almost any prior click. The first understates results and starves good campaigns. The second overstates results and rewards campaigns that are coasting on demand the brand already had.

A few signs suggest the window is mismatched to the cycle:
  • The setting still reads as the default across ad sets with very different products or sales cycles
  • The average time from first ad interaction to purchase, visible in conversion reporting, runs longer than the click window
  • The same window is applied to a fast impulse purchase and a long-consideration purchase in one account
You may want to compare the click window against the real time-to-purchase for each campaign's offer before deciding the window is right. The default is a starting point, not a match to any one business.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the buying cycle varies by price and category. An impulse item often converts within a day of the click, while a considered purchase can take a week or more of comparison shopping. You may want to read the time-to-purchase for each store's catalog and set the click window to cover the typical cycle, rather than running one default window across products that sell on very different timelines.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the conversion is a form fill or a call, and the gap between the ad interaction and the lead can be short. A long click window then risks crediting the ad for leads that arrived through other channels. You may want to confirm the window reflects how quickly the audience submits after seeing an ad, and consider a shorter click window when leads convert close to the interaction.

learn_more.source_title: About attribution windows in Meta Ads Manager

investigate.intro: Read the attribution setting on each ad set, then compare the click window against the typical time from ad interaction to conversion for that campaign's offer.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open Ads Manager and add the 'Attribution setting' column to the reporting table so the window for each ad set is visible at a glance.
Scan the column across ad sets and note any account where one default window covers products or offers with clearly different sales cycles.
For a sample of campaigns, open the breakdown by time and read how long after the ad interaction conversions tend to land. Compare that to the click window in the setting.

meta_events_manager.label: Meta Events Manager
steps: Open Events Manager and confirm the dataset is recording the conversion events the campaigns optimize toward, so the time-to-conversion you read in Ads Manager rests on events that are actually firing.
Note whether the key purchase or lead event shows recent, steady activity rather than gaps that would make any window comparison unreliable.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad set, the attribution setting in use and the typical time from ad interaction to conversion for that offer. An ad set whose click window comfortably covers the typical time-to-conversion, set deliberately rather than left at the default, passes. An ad set on the default window where the typical time-to-conversion falls outside that window, or where one window spans clearly different sales cycles, is a fail recorded with the ad set name and the observed time-to-conversion.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the attribution setting and the typical time-to-conversion for each ad set above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that ad sets on {{site_name}} are running the default attribution window, which does not match the buying cycle of the products they promote. The window decides both which conversions Meta reports and which conversions feed delivery, so a mismatch distorts the count and steers optimization toward the wrong signal at the same time. {{voice_recommend}} reading the typical time from ad interaction to conversion for each offer and setting the click window to cover that cycle, rather than leaving the default in place across campaigns that sell on different timelines. After the change, watch how reported conversion volume and cost per result shift once the window reflects how the audience actually buys.

audit_checks.title: Meta-reported conversions are compared against another tool without matching the attribution window
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Meta and a separate analytics or ad tool each apply their own attribution model and window, so a side-by-side comparison that ignores the difference reads as a tracking failure when it is really two methods measuring on different terms. Decisions made on that false gap, such as cutting a campaign or rebuilding a tag, waste effort and can pause spend that was working.

educate.base:
Meta credits a conversion using its own attribution setting, the click window and view window on the ad set, and its own modeling for conversions it cannot observe directly. A separate tool, such as a web analytics platform or another ad platform, applies a different model and a different window. When the two numbers are placed side by side without accounting for those differences, the gap looks like one tool is wrong, when the two are simply counting on different terms.

Three differences drive most of the gap. The attribution model differs, in that Meta credits views and clicks within its window while a last-click analytics report may credit only the final click. The window length differs, so a 7-day click setting in Meta captures conversions a same-session analytics view never would. The conversion definition differs too, in that the event Meta optimizes toward and the goal the other tool reports may not be the same action.

Reading the gap as a tracking failure leads to the wrong fixes. A practitioner re-checks the pixel, rebuilds the tag, or disputes the platform, when the data is functioning and the comparison was unfair. The more useful move is to align what can be aligned and accept that some difference is structural.

A few steps make the comparison fair:
  • Match the window where both tools allow it, for example reporting Meta on the same lookback the analytics tool uses
  • Compare the same conversion event in both tools, not a purchase in one and a broader goal in the other
  • Treat a residual gap as the expected result of two attribution methods, not as a number to reconcile to zero
You may want to document the window and model each tool uses before drawing any conclusion from a difference. A gap explained by method is not a defect to fix.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the comparison is usually Meta purchases against purchases in a web analytics tool or the platform's own reporting. The analytics tool often credits the last click, so it gives Meta less credit for view-driven and assisted sales. You may want to align the lookback windows and compare the same purchase event before treating the difference as a measurement problem on the store.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the comparison is often Meta leads against form submissions recorded in a customer relationship management (CRM) tool or analytics. The CRM may log a lead only after a qualifying step, while Meta counts the submission, so the two count different moments. You may want to confirm both tools are measuring the same action before reading a gap as a tracking fault.

learn_more.source_title: Compare attribution settings in Meta Ads reporting

investigate.intro: Identify the attribution model and window each tool uses, align them where the tools allow, and confirm both are counting the same conversion event.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Add the 'Attribution setting' column and note the window applied to the ad sets being compared.
Use the comparison view in Meta Ads reporting to see results under more than one attribution setting at once, which shows how much of the gap is window-driven.
Confirm the conversion event reported in Meta matches the event the other tool reports, rather than a different action with a similar name.

web_analytics.label: Web analytics or other tool
steps: Find the attribution model the other tool applies, for example last click, and the lookback window it reports on.
Where the tool allows it, set its window to match Meta's click window so both numbers cover the same span.
Compare the same conversion event across both tools and record the residual gap that remains after the windows are aligned.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per compared metric, the attribution model and window each tool applies, whether the windows were aligned, and whether the same event was compared in both. A comparison where the windows and the event are matched, with any residual difference attributed to method, passes. A comparison drawing a conclusion from a raw gap between mismatched windows or different events is a fail recorded with the two tools and the metric compared.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the attribution model, window, and conversion event for each tool being compared above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Meta conversions are being compared against another tool without accounting for the different attribution windows and models, so a structural difference is being read as a tracking failure. Meta credits clicks and views within its window and models conversions it cannot observe, while the other tool applies its own model and window, and the two will not match by design. {{voice_recommend}} aligning the lookback windows where both tools allow it, comparing the same conversion event in each, and treating the remaining difference as the expected result of two methods rather than a number to reconcile to zero. After that, decisions can rest on a like-for-like comparison instead of a gap that was never a defect.

audit_checks.title: View-through credit is misread as click-driven performance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: View-through conversions credit an ad that was seen but not clicked, and they sit in the same conversion total as click-through conversions unless they are separated out. Reading a total inflated by view-through credit as click-driven demand can keep budget on campaigns whose apparent results lean on impressions the buyer may never have noticed.

educate.base:
The view window in an attribution setting, for example the '1-day view' half of 7-day click and 1-day view, credits a conversion to an ad that a person saw but did not click, as long as the conversion happens within the view window. That view-through conversion lands in the same conversion total as a click-through conversion. Read at the level of the total, the two are indistinguishable, so a campaign earning most of its credit from views can look as productive as one earning credit from clicks.

View-through credit is weaker evidence of ad influence than a click. A click is a deliberate action. A view counts an impression the person may have scrolled past without registering. Crediting a conversion to an impression assumes the impression mattered, and for some placements and audiences that assumption is generous.

The practical risk is reading an inflated total as click-driven demand. A campaign with a large share of view-through conversions can show a strong cost per result while contributing little incremental action beyond what the audience would have done anyway. Budget then stays on apparent performance that thins out under scrutiny.

A few moves separate view-through credit from click-driven results:
  • Report click-through and view-through conversions separately rather than reading only the combined total
  • Note campaigns whose results lean heavily on the view window, especially broad awareness placements
  • Consider whether the view window length fits the placement, given that a long view window credits more impressions as conversions
You may want to split the two before judging a campaign's contribution. A total that looks strong can rest on view-through credit that incremental testing would not support.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, view-through credit can attach to shoppers who would have purchased regardless, given that a returning buyer is likely to see and convert without the ad changing the outcome. You may want to separate view-through from click-through purchases and look at whether the view-credited sales concentrate among existing customers before reading the combined total as new demand.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a view-through lead credits an ad the person saw before submitting a form they may have reached another way. You may want to report click-through and view-through leads separately and weigh whether the view-credited leads reflect ad influence or demand the brand already had.

learn_more.source_title: About attribution models and attribution settings
Compare attribution settings in Meta Ads reporting

investigate.intro: Separate view-through conversions from click-through conversions in reporting and identify campaigns whose results lean on the view window.

meta_ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: In Ads Manager, use the comparison view to show results under a click-only setting alongside the click-and-view setting, which exposes how much of each campaign's total comes from the view window.
Note campaigns where the conversion total drops sharply once the view window is removed, which marks those that lean on view-through credit.
Check the view window length on those ad sets and consider whether it fits the placement and the offer.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the conversion total under the click-and-view setting and under a click-only comparison, then notes the share that comes from the view window. A campaign whose results hold up with view-through credit removed, or whose view share is understood and accepted, passes. A campaign whose apparent performance falls sharply without view-through credit, read until now as click-driven, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the view-driven share.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the click-only and click-and-view conversion totals for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is reading conversion totals that include view-through credit as if they were click-driven results. The view window credits ads that were seen but not clicked, and that credit sits in the same total as click-through conversions, so a campaign leaning on views can look as strong as one earning clicks. {{voice_recommend}} reporting click-through and view-through conversions separately, identifying campaigns whose results fall sharply once view credit is removed, and weighing the view window length against the placement. After the split, budget can follow results that hold up rather than totals propped up by impressions the buyer may never have registered.
